role_description: Instrument Lead responsible for coordinating multiple instrument crews on an industrial construction project. Assigns work to senior techs and techs, ensures loop diagrams and hook-up drawings are followed, coordinates with other trades for access and tie-ins, tracks instrument installation and calibration progress, and reports to the instrument supervisor.
report_focus: When structuring this person's reports, look for: crew assignments, installation progress by area, loop check completion, calibration activities, coordination with electrical and piping, material and tool needs, quality issues found, safety observations, problems escalated from techs, and plans for tomorrow.

vocabulary.description: Management and technical terms used by an instrument lead coordinating crews, tracking progress, and interfacing with supervision.

safety_rules: All personnel must wear required PPE at all times: hard hat, safety glasses, steel-toe boots, FR clothing
Conduct crew-level JSA reviews before starting each new task or entering a new work area
Lead daily toolbox talks covering task-specific hazards and site conditions
Ensure all crew members have completed required training and orientations before starting work
Verify LOTO isolation is in place before authorizing any work on energized or pressurized systems
Never allow work to proceed if safety conditions are not met — exercise stop work authority
Ensure fall protection is used at heights of 6 feet or more — verify crew tie-off compliance
Verify scaffold inspection tags are current before allowing crew to use scaffolds
Ensure confined space permits are active and an attendant is posted before entry
Hot work permits must be obtained and a fire watch assigned before any welding or grinding
Report all incidents, near-misses, and unsafe conditions to the supervisor immediately
Ensure proper area classification compliance — verify IS barriers and XP equipment are in place
Monitor crew fatigue levels — do not allow fatigued workers to operate lifts or perform critical tasks
Ensure all calibration gas cylinders are properly secured and stored
Verify intrinsically safe equipment is being used in classified areas
Coordinate with other trades to identify and mitigate multi-trade hazards
Ensure new hires and transferred personnel receive area-specific hazard orientation
Maintain accurate crew safety documentation including training records and certifications
Know the location of emergency equipment — fire extinguisher, eye wash, AED, safety shower
Ensure crews follow proper lifting procedures and use mechanical aids when available
tools_and_equipment: Fluke multimeter — for troubleshooting and verification of crew work
Fluke process calibrator — mA source/measure for loop verification
Beamex MC6 calibrator — documenting calibrator for auditable calibration records
HART communicator — for smart instrument configuration and diagnostics
Laptop computer — for DCS/PLC software, documentation, progress tracking, and email
Tablet or iPad — for digital work packages, drawings, and field markup
Two-way radio — for crew coordination and emergency communication
Scheduling software — Primavera P6, Microsoft Project, or site-specific tools
Spreadsheets — for tracking loop check completion, calibrations, and material status
Digital camera — for documenting installations, deficiencies, and as-built conditions
Drawing sets — P&IDs, loop diagrams, hook-up drawings, cable schedules
Instrument index — master list of all instruments, tags, and installation status
Punch list forms — for documenting and tracking deficiencies
QC inspection forms — for documenting quality hold points and inspections
Torque wrench — calibrated, for verifying crew torque on critical connections
Pressure test equipment — for witnessing and verifying tubing pressure tests
Megger / insulation tester — for cable acceptance testing oversight
Label maker — for ensuring proper cable and instrument identification
Level and measuring tools — for verifying instrument mounting per spec
PPE inspection kit — for verifying crew PPE compliance
language_notes: Workers may mix English and Spanish. Technical terms and tag numbers are usually said in English even in Spanish-language reports. Management terms like schedule, progress, and percent complete are often in English. Common Spanish terms: 'cuadrilla' (crew), 'avance' (progress), 'material', 'falta' (shortage), 'problema' (problem).
